title: “Sunakkhatta, I give this simile to make the meaning clear. The meaning here is this: The wound, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the six sense-bases…
content: “Sunakkhatta, I give this simile to make the meaning clear. The meaning here is this: The wound, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the six sense-bases (phassayatana). The poison, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for ignorance. The dart, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for craving. The probing tool, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for mindfulness. The knife, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for noble wisdom. The surgeon, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. Indeed, Sunakkhatta, a monk who guards himself in regard to the six sense-bases, knowing that ‘Attachment (upadhi) is the root of suffering,’ becomes unattached and liberated by the destruction of attachment. It does not happen that he would still focus his body on attachment or direct his mind to attachment.”

title: “Monks, just as a defiled, dust-covered cloth, when a dyer dips it in this or that dye – blue, yellow, red, or purple – will…
content: “Monks, just as a defiled, dust-covered cloth, when a dyer dips it in this or that dye – blue, yellow, red, or purple – will take the dye poorly and be unclean. Why is that? Because the cloth is not clean. So too, monks, a foul destination awaits a defiled mind. Monks, just as a clean, bright cloth, when a dyer dips it in this or that dye – blue, yellow, red, or purple – will take the dye well and be clean. Why is that? Because the cloth is clean. So too, monks, a good destination awaits an undefiled mind.”

title: “Monks, if anyone would develop these four foundations of mindfulness for just seven days, one of two fruits could be expected: either final knowledge here…
content: “Monks, if anyone would develop these four foundations of mindfulness for just seven days, one of two fruits could be expected: either final knowledge here and now, or if there remains a trace of clinging, the state of non-return. Monks, this is the one-way path for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow and lamentation, for the disappearance of pain and grief, for the attainment of the true way, for the realization of Nibbāna – namely, the Four Foundations of Mindfulness.”

title: Elder Sunāga: “Skilled in the mind’s signs, Knowing the taste of seclusion, Meditating, mindful, careful, He attains the non-worldly happiness.”
content:
Elder Sunāga:
“Skilled in the mind’s signs,
Knowing the taste of seclusion,
Meditating, mindful, careful,
He attains the non-worldly happiness.”

title: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in those forms,…
content: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then, monks, this is called a monk who has gone into Māra’s domain, who has come under Māra’s control. Māra’s net has surrounded him. Bound by Māra, he is subject to the Evil One’s will. Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye … If a monk does not delight in those forms, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them — then, monks, this is called a monk who has not gone into Māra’s domain, not come under Māra’s control. Māra’s net has not surrounded him. Freed from Māra’s bondage, he is not subject to the Evil One’s will.”

title: Sitting down to one side, the Venerable Rādha said to the Blessed One: “Good, venerable sir, if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma…
content: Sitting down to one side, the Venerable Rādha said to the Blessed One: “Good, venerable sir, if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief. Having heard the Dhamma from the Blessed One, I will dwell alone, secluded, heedful, ardent, and resolute.” - “Rādha, whatever is impermanent — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Rādha, is impermanent? The eye, Rādha, is impermanent — there you should abandon your desire. Forms are impermanent … Eye-consciousness … Eye-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is impermanent. There you should abandon your desire. The ear … The nose … The tongue … The body … The mind is impermanent — there you should abandon your desire. Phenomena … Mind-consciousness … Mind-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on mind-contact — pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant — that feeling is impermanent. There you should abandon your desire. Whatever is impermanent, Rādha — there you should abandon your desire.”

title: “And what, Ānanda, is the path, the way leading to the abandoning of the five lower fetters? Here, Ānanda, a monk, by the relinquishment of…
content: “And what, Ānanda, is the path, the way leading to the abandoning of the five lower fetters? Here, Ānanda, a monk, by the relinquishment of attachments (upadhiviveka), by the abandoning of unwholesome states, by the complete calming of bodily coarseness, secluded from sensual desires, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion.”

title: “Monk, ‘ant-hill’ is a synonym for this body composed of the four great elements, born of father and mother, nourished with rice and porridge, impermanent,…
content: “Monk, ‘ant-hill’ is a synonym for this body composed of the four great elements, born of father and mother, nourished with rice and porridge, impermanent, subject to rubbing, pressing, breaking, disintegration, dissolution. ‘Smoking by day and blazing by night’—what belongs to daytime work, reflected upon at night—that is ‘smoking by night.’ What, after reflecting at night, is practiced during the day by body, speech, and mind—that is ‘blazing by day.’ ‘Brahmin’ is a synonym for the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. ‘Wise man’ is a synonym for a monk in higher training. ‘Sword’ is a synonym for noble wisdom. ‘Digging up’ is a synonym for arousing effort, striving. ‘Door-bolt’ is a synonym for ignorance; ‘lifting the door-bolt’ means abandoning that ignorance. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning.”

title: “Monks, one who dwells seeing gratification in things that are fetters — greed is not abandoned, hatred is not abandoned, delusion is not abandoned. Because…
content: “Monks, one who dwells seeing gratification in things that are fetters — greed is not abandoned, hatred is not abandoned, delusion is not abandoned. Because greed, hatred, and delusion are not abandoned, he is not freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is not freed from suffering.”

title: Few among humans Reach the further shore, While the rest of humankind Run up and down on this shore. Those who practice the Dhamma In…
content:
Few among humans
Reach the further shore,
While the rest of humankind
Run up and down on this shore.
Those who practice the Dhamma
In accordance with the well-taught Dhamma,
Will reach the further shore,
Beyond the realm of death, so hard to escape.
The wise, having left the dark state,
Should develop the bright,
Leaving home for the homeless life,
Living in seclusion, where pleasure is hard to find.
Seeking delight in Nibbāna,
Having abandoned sensual pleasures, without possessions,
The wise should cleanse themselves
Of defilements of the mind.
Those who have well developed the factors of enlightenment
With a mind rightly directed,
Who, without clinging, delight in the abandoning of grasping —
They, the defilement-free, the radiant,
Attain final Nibbāna in the world.
